GAF.value_prop: LayerLock helps resist wind uplift—common choice for {{city}} wind seasons.

Owens_Corning.value_prop: SureNail strip adds grip through fastener lines—useful when winds spike near {{neighborhood}}.

CertainTeed.value_prop: Heavier laminate lines can look richer on street-facing planes in {{city}}.

TAMKO.value_prop: Reinforced zones for weather events; budget-conscious {{county}} projects.

Hip.benefit: Often more wind-stable; insurers sometimes note hip roofs favorably.

Mansard.benefit: More attic volume; steep visibility means shingle quality shows.
Mansard.aoe_hint: Premium aesthetics matter on high-visibility {{neighborhood}} streets.

Flat.aoe_hint: Check {{city}} code for low-slope minimum slopes.

asphalt.notes: Typical architectural laminate range for {{city}} neighborhoods.

metal_standing_seam.notes: Long-life option; great when hail risk is a priority in {{county}}.

clay_tile.notes: Heavier; verify structure—more common in some {{state}} markets.

synthetic_slate.notes: Luxury look at lower weight than natural slate.

aoe_hint: Hail often dents metal flashings first—if vents are dinged, schedule a close shingle inspection.
Replacement ranges swing with decking repairs, steep pitch, and layers—always get line-item quotes.
If a claim is unclear, documented photos + a local inspection report help secondary reviews.
Patching very old shingles can disturb adjacent tabs—ask whether partial vs full replacement is safer.
